bibliographic_data.title: Scalable Mechanisms for Rational Secret Sharing
bibliographic_data.abstract: We consider the classical secret sharing problem in the case where all agents are selﬁsh but rational. In recent work, Kol and Naor show that, when there are two players, in the non-simultaneous communication model, i.e., when rushing is possible, there is no Nash equilibrium that ensures both players learn the secret. However, they describe a mechanism for this problem, for any number of players, that is an ϵ-Nash equilibrium, in that no player can gain more than ϵ utility by deviating from it. Unfortunately, the Kol and Naor mechanism, and, to the best of our knowledge, all previous mechanisms for this problem require each agent to send O(n) messages in expectation, where n is the number of agents. This may be problematic for some applications of rational secret sharing such as secure multi-party computation and simulation of a mediator. We address this issue by describing mechanisms for rational secret sharing that are designed for large n. Both of our results hold for n ≥3, and are Nash equilibria, rather than just ϵ-Nash equilbria. Our first result is a mechanism for n-out-of-n rational secret sharing that is scalable in the sense that it requires each agent to send only an expected O(log n) bits. Moreover, the latency of this mechanism is O(log n) in expectation, compared to O(n) expected latency for the Kol and Naor result. Our second result is a mechanism for a relaxed variant of rational m-out-of-n secret sharing where m = Θ(n). It requires each processor to send O(log n) bits and has O(log n) latency. Both of our mechanisms are non-cryptographic, and are not susceptible to backwards induction.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents scalable mechanisms for rational secret sharing that require each agent to send only an expected O(log n) bits, compared to the previous mechanism which requires O(n) messages.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors design two mechanisms: one for n-out-of-n rational secret sharing and another for a relaxed variant of m-out-of-n rational secret sharing where m = Θ(n). Both mechanisms ensure Nash equilibria and have logarithmic latency and communication complexity.
outcomes: A scalable mechanism for n-out-of-n rational secret sharing that requires each agent to send only an expected O(log n) bits.
A mechanism for a relaxed variant of m-out-of-n rational secret sharing where m = Θ(n) that also requires each processor to send O(log n) bits and has O(log n) latency.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, we address the issue of scalable mechanisms for rational secret sharing. The classical problem involves distributing shares of a secret among n selﬁsh but rational agents such that any group of m agents can reconstruct the secret while knowledge of fewer than m shares reveals nothing about it. We design two main results: (1) A mechanism for n-out-of-n rational secret sharing where each agent sends only an expected O(log n) bits, and has a latency of O(log n). This is compared to previous mechanisms which require O(n) messages in expectation. (2) A mechanism for a relaxed variant of m-out-of-n rational secret sharing where m = Θ(n), also requiring each processor to send O(log n) bits with O(log n) latency. Both mechanisms are non-cryptographic and not susceptible to backwards induction, making them suitable for large networks in applications like secure multi-party computation.

nodes.label: m-out-of-n rational secret sharing mechanism (m = Θ(n))
